content: {"perspective": "Public_Health", "question": "Round 1 established that the NHANES short-eating-window signal and the Ramadan observational literature disagree on long-term cardiovascular risk, with reverse causation as a likely reconciliation. Shift focus to generalizability: the RCT literature on IF for metabolic syndrome is overwhelmingly drawn from Western, high-income, research-setting populations. What evidence exists on the effectiveness and safety of intermittent fasting — including Ramadan and non-Ramadan protocols — in sub-Saharan African, South Asian, and Latin American cohorts with metabolic syndrome, and what baseline cardiometabolic, nutritional, and food-environment differences would be expected to modify the RCT effect sizes in those populations?", "search_queries": ["intermittent fasting metabolic syndrome sub-Saharan Africa randomized controlled trial cohort", "Ramadan fasting metabolic syndrome South Asia India Pakistan Bangladesh cardiometabolic", "intermittent fasting Latin America Mexico Brazil prediabetes lifestyle intervention", "Ramadan fasting type 2 diabetes sub-Saharan Africa nutritional adherence Muslim population", "low middle income country LMIC intermittent fasting generalizability food insecurity metabolic"]}
